text: Ever since Disney acquired Aardman, we've been forced to endure Aardman mouth animation - big round ovals.  It's tiresome, looks terrible, leaves everything puffy-mouthed, and the lack of articulation doesn't naturally map to the voices making the vocal sync feel off even when it isn't.  The only benefit of that animation is it's simpler and  cheaper to produce.  This also feels like they tried to go so out-there with the design they're about to repeat the mistake of "Strange World" (a movie so forgettable I had to look up the name despite having seen it) - which honestly, this looks like it was made by the same crew.  And what happened to "Pixar doesn't do sequels"?  Of the five upcoming ones including this, three are sequels.  At least this one isn't.

text: Yes, it's an original screenplay. But this looks painfully average and derivative and gives off the "I've seen this story a thousand times before" vibe. Do you remember when Pixar used to make, you know, films that felt different from each other? If you look at Monster's Inc, Ratatouille and Wall-E for example, those were truly some fantastic films that felt not only original but UNIQUE, like each was telling a new story. This film however follows the same basic setup that Pixar films have done in recent years: small kid misfit who wants to fit in and ends up on a life-changing fantastical journey. So remember, just because a screenplay is original does not make it unique nor fresh.
